steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers concluded that net CO² is likely to increase when seasonal snow arrives earlier relative to the onset of soil surface freezing. A supporting finding must explain the mechanism: why does early snow drive net CO² up? The passage tells us CO² rises when heterotrophic respiration (output) outpaces plant absorption (uptake). The correct choice must show that early snow favors respiration over plant growth.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'early snow reduces moisture and lowers heterotrophic respiration': lower respiration would reduce CO² output, not increase it — this contradicts the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice B — 'snow insulation lets respiration continue while plants like O. secunda are not yet growing': if snow arrives before soil freezes, it insulates the soil and keeps it warm enough for soil microbes to keep releasing CO², while plants remain dormant and cannot absorb it. Output continues; uptake does not. Net CO² rises. This directly supports the conclusion. ✓
  • Choice C — 'soil moisture effects are stronger during snow cover than without': addresses the relative strength of soil-moisture effects, not the timing of snow arrival and its impact on the respiration–growth balance. ✗
  • Choice D — 'snow persists longer in low-vegetation, high-respiration areas': about where snow lingers, not when it arrives — irrelevant to the conclusion about early snow arrival. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
